title: Atom Interferometry with Transverse Optical Modes
authors: Ryan Husband, Ryan J. Thomas, Yosri Ben-Aïcha, Rhys H. Eagle, Jessica Eastman, John E. Debs, Patrick J. Everitt, Michael Larsen, Eric Imhof, Charles A. Sackett, John D. Close, Simon A. Haine, Samuel Legge
abstract: We experimentally demonstrate atom interferometry using the transverse phase profile of an optical mode. As proof-of-principle, we use the helical phase windings of Hypergeometric Gaussian beams for Ramsey interferometry with ensembles of ballistically-expanding cold Rb87 atoms, and we show that the interferometer can measure rotations induced by a motor with a sensitivity that scales linearly with orbital angular momentum and interferometer time. We characterize the thermal decoherence of the interferometer, deriving and experimentally confirming a closed-form expression for the spatially-varying interferometer visibility arising near the singularity of the helical phase winding, motivating the use of condensed atoms in ring-shaped traps.
summary: This paper demonstrates atom interferometry using the transverse phase structure of optical beams, specifically utilizing the helical phase windings of HyGG beams. The authors successfully measured rotations and provided a detailed theoretical and experimental characterization of how thermal motion causes spatially-dependent decoherence in the interferometer contrast. This advances the field by establishing a robust method for high-precision sensing using structured light.
main_problem: To develop and experimentally demonstrate atom interferometry utilizing the transverse phase profile (orbital angular momentum) of structured optical modes, moving beyond traditional longitudinal phase measurements.
main_result: The interferometer successfully measures rotations with a sensitivity scaling linearly with the orbital angular momentum quantum number ($\ell$) and interrogation time ($t_{	ext{int}}$). Furthermore, the study derived and confirmed an analytical expression for the spatially-varying thermal decoherence of the fringe visibility.
method: The authors employed Ramsey interferometry with cold $^{87}	ext{Rb}$ atoms, using Hypergeometric Gaussian (HyGG) beams to imprint helical phase windings. They measured rotation by inducing physical rotations of the optical fields and characterized decoherence by measuring fringe contrast reduction.
model_or_system: The physical system is atom interferometry using cold $^{87}	ext{Rb}$ atoms and structured light fields (HyGG beams). The theoretical analysis models the phase accumulation ($\Delta\phi$) and contrast ($C$) by considering the dependence on atomic velocities ($\dot{r}, \dot{	heta}$) in the presence of a helical phase front.
key_observables: Rotational phase shift ($\phi$), interferometer fringe contrast, and the measured dephasing radius ($R$).
important_parameters: Orbital Angular Momentum quantum number ($\ell$), interrogation time ($t_{	ext{int}}$), and the atomic temperature (which dictates decoherence).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations for the change in angle ($\Delta	heta$) during ballistic expansion and assumes that the primary limitation for sensitivity is thermal phase diffusion.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate measured Ramsey fringes showing dependence on $\ell$ and $\Omega$, and plots confirm the linear scaling of the measured dephasing radius ($R$) with the predicted analytical scaling law derived from the thermal motion.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from demonstrating the basic rotation measurement using transverse modes to quantitatively characterizing the thermal decoherence. It develops the theoretical framework by modeling the phase accumulation in polar coordinates, leading to the final analytical expression for the contrast reduction due to velocity spread.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it pushes the metrological capabilities of atom interferometry by systematically incorporating and quantifying the effects of transverse optical structure (OAM) and thermal decoherence, providing a roadmap for next-generation quantum sensors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Measured Ramsey fringes from a LPAI with an optical vortex, showing the increase in fringe frequency with ℓ. The interferograms in the top two rows illustrate how the helical phase structure of the optical vortex rotates in the lab frame, with red arrows indicating the angle of rotation and white text indicating the corresponding phase change of the Ramsey fringe, ϕ, from Eq. (1).

FIG. 2. (top) The cold atom and interferometer apparatus as described in the text, where the dashed box labeled (a) or (b) indicates the two different optical vortex generation options. (a) A Gaussian beam becomes a HyGG mode by helical phase modulation using an acrylic spiral phase plate (SPP). The phase plate is mounted to a rotation stage (RS) [63] and set to rotate about a fixed axis by a controller. The HyGG mode is then magnified to cover the atom cloud (to scale). (b) A Gaussian beam retro-reflects from a spatial light modulator (SLM) [64] set as a SPP. A 4f imaging system ensures that the intensity profile of the Gaussian beam incident on the SLM is positioned onto the atom cloud...

FIG. 3. (a–b) Measured interferometer phases ϕ(Ω) due to rotation of the phase plate (markers) with linear fits (solid lines), with the fit offset subtracted for clarity. Measurements for different ℓand a fixed T = 1 ms are shown in (a) and for different T and a fixed ℓ= 300 in (b). (c) Extracted slope, ϕ/(Ωteff), from (a) as a function of ℓ. Solid line is a linear fit to ϕ/Ωteff = 1.01(0.01)ℓ−5.6(5.9). (d) Extracted slope, ϕ/(Ωℓ), from (b) as a function of teff. Solid line is a linear fit to ϕ/Ωℓ= 1.01(0.02)teff + 6.5(16) µs.

FIG. 4. (a) Schematic illustration of the effect of thermal motion of the atoms on the interferometer contrast. Atoms are initially imprinted with a helical phase (shown as variation in colour), and the atoms in the center of the cloud move more than those at the edges, obscuring the interferometer phase and leading to contrast reduction. (b) The dephasing radius R measured as a function of √σrσvℓtint over all ℓ, tint, and T. The markers are the measured data, and the black solid line is Eq. (2) with no free parameters and with the gray shaded area showing the one standard deviation model uncertainty based on uncertainties in T and σr. (c) An example of the central dip in fringe amplitude...

FIG. 5. A single thermal atom (red circle) is pictured at radius r moving at velocity v across the helical phase front, ϕ, of a GV mode (ℓ= 5) in the VMG. During tint, the atom will exhibit an interference phase ∆ϕ, determined by the immediate direction of motion (the colored ring). The dephasing region radius R is shown in blue.
